Well, Aman sold 100% of his Nike shares, but what he actually did was convert that into call options instead. Aman still believes Nike is a turnaround play. Aman said that Nike has made three mistakes which has caused erosion of shareholder value. Number one, they switched to focusing on a direct to consumer strategy at the expense of wholesale partnerships. Number two, a merchandising structure that did not prioritize sports. And number three, overproduction of bestselling franchises in place of working harder on innovation. The third is best summed up as resting on their laurels. What all of that actually means is Nike is not focusing on the core business that made them great. His rationale for converting to options instead was this, and I quote, "In a successful turnaround, the option payoffs should be more than double the returns from owning the common stock." We continue to believe that Nike has the potential to be one of the great large cap consumer turnarounds.

What we can see is Pepsi now has a starting dividend yield of around 3.63%. And over the last 5 and 10 years, they've been growing at a rate of about 6 to 7%. But upon closer look, you will probably notice there's a problem here. The free cash flow payout ratio in the most recent year was 100%. So 100% every single dollar of Pepsi's free cash flow had to be used to pay out a dividend. This isn't a good sign because we have to remember when it comes to capital allocation, a company has five options. They can reinvest back into the business. They can pay down debt. They can buy back shares, attempt mergers and acquisitions, and then of course pay out a dividend.
